name: Puranic
description: Trimurti se Trishul tak — har number ke peeche ek katha.

earlyFive.phrase: Paanch Pandav taiyaar — Early Five ho gaya!

topLine.phrase: Pehli lakeer paar — Ganga utar aayi! Top Line!

middleLine.phrase: Beech ka raasta — Madhya Marg! Middle Line!

bottomLine.phrase: Aakhri lakeer, patal tak — Bottom Line!

fullHouse.phrase: Poora ticket, poori katha — FULL HOUSE! Aarti utaaro!

1.phrase: Ekam sat — sach ek hi hai, naam anek. Number 1, ek!
1.note: Rig Veda 1.164.46, 'ekam sat vipra bahudha vadanti' — the single most quoted line in Hindu thought. Opens the game on a hush, then the room wakes up. Best possible first call.

2.phrase: Aadha Shiv, aadhi Parvati — ek mein do. Number 2, do!
2.note: Ardhanarishvara — the half-Shiva half-Parvati form. Genuinely two-in-one, so the number is load-bearing rather than decorative.

3.phrase: Brahma, Vishnu, Mahesh — trishul ke teen phal. Number 3, teen!
3.note: Trimurti + the trident's three prongs. Two independent threes stacked; everyone in the room has one of these on a wall.

4.phrase: Char Ved, char yug, Brahma ke char mukh. Number 4, char!
4.note: Triple-stacked: Vedas, yugas, Brahma's four faces. Rapid-fire list delivery works well here.

5.phrase: Paanch Pandav, paanch tatva. Number 5, paanch!
5.note: Pandavas + the five elements. Universal recognition floor — nobody misses this one.

6.phrase: Kartikeya ke chhah mukh — ya insaan ke chhah dushman. Number 6, chhah!
6.note: Shanmukha (six-faced Kartikeya) + the shadripu — kaam, krodh, lobh, moh, mad, matsar. The pivot on 'ya' is the joke.

7.phrase: Saat pheras, saat vachan — aur aasman mein Saptarishi. Number 7, saat!
7.note: Seven wedding vows + the seven sages / Ursa Major. Every married person in the room reacts to the first half. Hero call.

8.phrase: Ashta siddhi nau nidhi ke daata — Hanuman ji ki aath siddhi. Number 8, aath!
8.note: Paraphrases a widely-known Hanuman Chalisa idea WITHOUT quoting the verse — original wording, same recognition. Note it sets up 9 (nau nidhi) if 9 comes later.

9.phrase: Nau Durga, nau grah, nau nidhi. Number 9, nau!
9.note: Triple-stack, all genuine. Navratri makes this seasonally hot.

10.phrase: Dus avatar Vishnu ke, dus sar Ravan ke. Number 10, dus!
10.note: Dashavatar vs Dashanan — hero and villain sharing a number. The symmetry is the joke. One of the best in the pack.

11.phrase: Gyarah Rudra — ya do khambe khade. Number 11, gyarah!
11.note: Ekadash Rudra + the shape joke. Deliberately replaces traditional housie's 'legs eleven' — same shape mechanic, family-safe.

12.phrase: Barah Jyotirling — Somnath se Rameshwaram. Number 12, barah!
12.note: Twelve Jyotirlingas. Naming the first and last is what makes it land — anyone who's done the circuit lights up.

13.phrase: Trayodashi ki raat — Shiv ka pradosh. Number 13, terah!
13.note: Thirteenth lunar day, sacred to Shiva. Deliberately NOT an unlucky-13 joke — that's a Western import and it lands flat here.

14.phrase: Chaudah saal ka vanvaas — aur Ram ji laut aaye. Number 14, chaudah!
14.note: The fourteen years of exile. This is the single strongest REF available to the theme — the whole festival of Diwali is this number. Save your best animation for it.

15.phrase: Pandrah tithi ke baad chand poora. Number 15, pandrah!
15.note: Fifteen tithis from amavasya to purnima.

16.phrase: Solah shringar — aur Krishan ji ki solah kalaayein. Number 16, solah!
16.note: The sixteen adornments + Krishna as shodasha-kala-purna (complete in all sixteen arts). Every woman at the table knows the first half cold.

17.phrase: Narad ji ka forward — 'satrah logon ko bhejo'. Number 17, satrah!
17.note: 17 has no genuine REF, so the cast fills it: Narad as the original chain-message forwarder. Anachronism is the joke. Everyone at the table has that uncle in the group.

18.phrase: Attharah adhyay Gita ke, attharah din ka yudh. Number 18, attharah!
18.note: Eighteen chapters of the Gita + eighteen days of Kurukshetra + eighteen Puranas. The densest true number in the theme.

19.phrase: Bees se ek kam — Ravan ki ek bhuja kat gayi. Number 19, unnees!
19.note: Sets up 20 (Ravana's twenty arms) or pays it off if 20 came first. Order-independent by design.

20.phrase: Bees bhujaayein Ravan ki. Number 20, bees!
20.note: Ten heads, twenty arms. Pairs with 19.

21.phrase: Ikkis baar Parashuram ne dharti naap di. Number 21, ikkis!
21.note: Parashurama's twenty-one campaigns. 'Naap di' softens the actual (genocidal) legend into something family-table appropriate.

22.phrase: Do hans, aamne-saamne — Saraswati ki sawaari. Number 22, bais!
22.note: The direct Indian answer to housie's 'two little ducks' — same digit-shape mechanic, but the swan is Saraswati's vahana so it stays inside the theme.

23.note: PLAIN — intentional. No genuine REF at 23 and the cast options were all strained. Keeping it bare is correct; a forced joke here costs more than it earns.

24.phrase: Chaubis akshar — Gayatri Mantra. Number 24, chaubis!
24.note: The Gayatri mantra has exactly twenty-four syllables. Also twenty-four avatars in the Bhagavata. Elders react hard to this one.

25.phrase: Paanch ke paanch guna — Pandavon ka poora kunba. Number 25, pachchees!
25.note: Thin — 5×5 dressed up. Working filler, no more.

27.phrase: Sattais nakshatra — aur Chandra ki sattais raniyaan. Number 27, sattais!
27.note: Twenty-seven nakshatras; Chandra married Daksha's twenty-seven daughters — genuinely one per nakshatra. Verified, lovely, and almost nobody at the table knows the second half. Hero call.

28.phrase: Sattais nakshatra… aur ek chhupa hua, Abhijit. Number 28, attaees!
28.note: Abhijit is the twenty-eighth nakshatra, dropped from most standard lists. True, obscure, and pays off 27 beautifully if it lands after it.

30.phrase: Tees din poore — Chitragupta ka hisaab band. Number 30, tees!
30.note: Chitragupta as the celestial accountant closing the month's books. The bureaucracy joke is the point.

32.phrase: Singhasan Battisi — battees putliyaan, battees kahaniyaan. Number 32, battees!
32.note: Vikramaditya's throne with thirty-two statuettes, each telling a story. Folk-tale canon rather than scripture — widens the pack beyond temple references. Grandparents light up.

33.phrase: Tetees koti devta — koti matlab prakaar, crore nahi! Number 33, tetees!
33.note: Thirty-three koti devatas, where 'koti' means TYPE, not crore — the most common misconception in Hindu numerics. The correction IS the joke and it starts an argument every single time. Hero call.

35.phrase: Teen aur paanch — Trimurti aur Pandav. Number 35, paintees!

36.phrase: Chhattees ka aankda — Ram aur Ravan wala rishta! Number 36, chhattees!
36.note: '36 ka aankda' is the standing Hindi idiom for mutual enmity — the digits face away from each other. Marrying the idiom to the theme's central rivalry is the cleanest joke in the pack. Hero call.

39.phrase: Chalis se ek kam — Chalisa abhi poora nahi hua. Number 39, unchalees!
39.note: Sets up 40. Works in either order.

40.phrase: Chalees chaupaiyaan — isi liye naam pada Chalisa. Number 40, chalees!
40.note: The Hanuman Chalisa is named for its forty verses — a fact half the room recites weekly without ever having noticed. That flash of 'oh!' is exactly what a hero call is for. No quoting of the text itself.

42.note: PLAIN — intentional. (In a FAMILY pack this slot is prime real estate for a custom line.)

44.phrase: Do chaukhat, aamne-saamne — mandir ke do dwaar. Number 44, chauwalees!
44.note: Traditional housie reads 4 as a door ('knock at the door'). Same shape mechanic, temple skin.

47.phrase: Char Ved, aur Saptarishi. Number 47, saintalees!
47.note: Thin — two REFs bolted together by digits. Acceptable filler.

49.phrase: Unchaas pawan — Maruton ki sena, Hanuman ka pitrakul. Number 49, unchaas!
49.note: The Maruts are counted as forty-nine wind deities; Hanuman is Pavanputra. A genuine deep cut — verified, and the one uncle who knows it will be delighted rather than argumentative.

50.phrase: Aadha safar poora — pachaas! Number 50, pachaas!
50.note: Halfway marker. Useful for pace — the room feels the game turn here.

51.phrase: Ikyavan Shakti Peeth — jahaan-jahaan Sati ke ang gire. Number 51, ikyavan!
51.note: Fifty-one Shakti Peethas from the Sati legend. Widely known, genuinely fifty-one, and the imagery is striking without being grim.

55.phrase: Do paanch — dono taraf Pandav khade! Number 55, pachpan!
55.note: Repeated digit + the theme's most famous five. Cheap but it works.

56.phrase: Chhappan bhog — Krishan ji ka thaal, ek nahi chhappan! Number 56, chhappan!
56.note: The fifty-six offerings. Universally known, food-adjacent, and reliably makes someone say they're hungry. Hero call.

60.phrase: Saath — jaise Ram aur Hanuman ka saath. Number 60, saath!
60.note: Perfect Hindi homophone: saath (sixty) / saath (togetherness). The only true pun in the pack and the theme supplies the most famous partnership going. Hero call — needs a slight pause before 'jaise' to land.

63.phrase: Chaunsath se ek kam — ek kala abhi baaki hai. Number 63, tirsath!

64.phrase: Chaunsath yogini, chaunsath kalaayein — Krishan ne saari seekh li. Number 64, chaunsath!
64.note: Sixty-four yoginis + the sixty-four classical arts. Both genuine, both sixty-four.

66.phrase: Do ankush, Ganesh ji ke haath mein. Number 66, chhiyasath!
66.note: The 6 read as the curved goad Ganesha holds. Doubles as a nice piece of iconography teaching.

68.phrase: Athsath tirth — sab Kashi mein sama gaye. Number 68, athsath!
68.note: The sixty-eight tirthas held to be gathered in Kashi. Real, and anyone from UP nods.

69.phrase: Ulta ya seedha — ek hi jaisa, Sheshnag ka chakkar! Number 69, unhattar!
69.note: Traditional housie's 'any way up' — rotational symmetry — reskinned as the coiled serpent. Deliberately CLEAN: this number's usual bingo treatment is exactly what we don't do.

73.phrase: Saat aur teen — Saptarishi aur Trimurti. Number 73, tihattar!

77.phrase: Do saat — Saptarishi do baar, aasman bhar gaya! Number 77, sattattar!
77.note: Repeated digit hook on the theme's strongest seven.

83.phrase: Chaurasi se ek kam — chakkar abhi poora nahi hua. Number 83, tirasi!

84.phrase: Chaurasi lakh yoni — chaurasi ka chakkar, sab isi se chhutkara maangte hain. Number 84, chaurasi!
84.note: The eighty-four lakh life forms one cycles through. 'Chaurasi ka chakkar' is live idiom, not just scripture — people say it about traffic. Hero call.

88.phrase: Do damru — Shiv ji ke haath mein, aur taal shuru! Number 88, athasi!
88.note: The damru IS an hourglass — literally the shape of an 8. This is the pack's proudest replacement: traditional housie's 88 is 'two fat ladies', which we will not use, and the damru is a strictly better joke on the same mechanic. Hero call.

89.phrase: Nabbe se ek kam — Kailash abhi ek kadam door. Number 89, nawasi!

90.phrase: Sabse ooncha — Kailash ki choti! Number 90, nabbe!
90.note: Top of the board = the highest peak. Direct thematic answer to housie's 'top of the shop'. NOTE FOR THE EDITOR: in a family pack, swap this for the warmest personal line you have — 'Dadi ki umar, aur abhi bhi sabse tez — nabbe!' — but ONLY if she'd laugh. This slot is the single best customization point in the pack; see guide §6.
